evals.prompt:
Generate an engineering report from these findings for our team and write it to engineering-report.md. We are deciding which message-queue technology to adopt for the event pipeline. Decision drivers: throughput >1M msg/s, at-least-once delivery, operational simplicity, and cloud-managed option available.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_technical_1 | title: Apache Kafka sustains 2M msg/s on a 3-broker cluster in benchmarks | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/kafka-bench-2026
- id: urn:mif:f_technical_2 | title: AWS SQS FIFO queues are capped at 3000 TPS per queue by default | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/aws-sqs-limits
- id: urn:mif:f_technical_3 | title: Redpanda Cloud offers a fully managed Kafka-compatible API with no ZooKeeper dependency | dimension: landscape | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/redpanda-cloud-2026
- id: urn:mif:f_technical_4 | title: Self-managed Kafka requires dedicated ops team averaging 0.5 FTE per cluster | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/kafka-ops-cost
evals.expected_output: A complete engineering report with all six required sections (Problem/Context, Options Considered, Trade-offs, Decision, Implementation Notes, Consequences), a markdown comparison table (options vs. criteria), front matter with title/date/status/decision-drivers, and inline numeric citations. No raw urn:mif: ids in the body prose.

deterministic_checks.description: Comparison table separator row present (markdown table required by spec)
Front matter status field (proposed / accepted / superseded) is present
Citations rendered as [N] markers or bare links — not raw urn:mif: ids

expectations.description: The body prose does not expose raw urn:mif:f_technical_N identifiers
expectations.criteria: None of the strings 'urn:mif:f_technical_1', 'urn:mif:f_technical_2', 'urn:mif:f_technical_3', or 'urn:mif:f_technical_4' appear in the rendered prose. They must be translated to [N] or footnote form.

expectations.description: The comparison table is an options-vs-criteria matrix, not a generic table
expectations.criteria: The markdown table has options (e.g. Kafka, SQS, Redpanda) as rows or columns and the decision drivers (throughput, at-least-once delivery, operational simplicity, cloud-managed) as the opposing axis. A table with only two columns or missing the decision-driver criteria does not satisfy this.

expectations.description: The decision is grounded in the comparison table — not stated without trade-off support
expectations.criteria: The Decision section names the chosen option and references the trade-offs or criteria from the comparison table as the rationale. A decision that appears without reference to the comparison table's evidence is not grounded.

expectations.description: Implementation Notes are actionable — an engineer could act from them
expectations.criteria: The Implementation Notes section contains at least one concrete action: a dependency to install, a migration step, an operational concern to address, or a rollout sequence. Generic guidance like 'evaluate your options' does not satisfy this.

evals.prompt:
Write an engineering decision report and write it to engineering-report.md. We're evaluating whether to use server-side rendering (SSR) or client-side rendering (CSR) for our new customer portal. Decision drivers: initial load time <2s on 3G, SEO indexability, team React expertise, time-to-interactive.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_perf_1 | title: SSR reduces time-to-first-byte by ~300ms vs CSR on 3G in controlled tests | dimension: performance | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/ssr-perf-study
- id: urn:mif:f_perf_2 | title: Next.js App Router supports incremental static regeneration and streaming SSR | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/nextjs-approuter
- id: urn:mif:f_perf_3 | title: Google confirmed Googlebot executes JavaScript for SPA indexing but with a crawl budget delay of 2-5 days | dimension: technical | verdict: weakened | citation: https://example.com/google-spa-indexing | note: disconfirming evidence suggests the crawl delay varies widely and may be longer for large SPAs
- id: urn:mif:f_perf_4 | title: CSR bundle hydration adds 800ms TTI on mid-range mobile for a 200KB JS payload | dimension: performance | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/csr-hydration-cost
evals.expected_output: Engineering report with all six required sections, a comparison table (SSR vs CSR on decision drivers), front matter, and inline citations. The weakened finding (f_perf_3) must be flagged as uncertain, not stated as settled fact. No falsified findings present but weakened one must carry a qualifier.

deterministic_checks.description: Markdown comparison table present (separator row check)

expectations.description: The weakened Googlebot crawl-delay finding is flagged inline as uncertain — not presented as settled fact
expectations.criteria: The claim about Googlebot crawl delay carries an inline qualifier ('may vary', 'unconfirmed', 'contested', 'weakened evidence', or similar). The finding should still appear — weakened means include-with-flag, not exclude.

expectations.description: The comparison table covers the stated decision drivers as criteria columns or rows
expectations.criteria: The comparison table addresses at least three of the four decision drivers: initial load time (<2s on 3G), SEO indexability, time-to-interactive, and team expertise. A table that only lists options without mapping to the criteria is insufficient.

expectations.description: The Decision section references specific trade-off evidence, not a generic preference
expectations.criteria: The Decision names the chosen approach (SSR or CSR) and ties it to concrete evidence from the findings — for example citing the 300ms TTFB advantage or the 800ms TTI hydration cost. A decision stated without referencing the trade-off data does not satisfy this.

evals.prompt:
Engineering decision doc please — write the report to engineering-report.md. Topic: choosing an observability backend for our microservices platform. Decision drivers: cost at 10TB/day ingest, OpenTelemetry native support, query latency <5s for last-24h spans, self-hosted option.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_obs_1 | title: Grafana Tempo stores traces at ~$0.02/GB/month with object storage backend | dimension: cost | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/grafana-tempo-cost
- id: urn:mif:f_obs_2 | title: Datadog APM charges per host plus a per-span ingestion fee that reaches $8k/month at 10TB/day | dimension: cost | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/datadog-pricing-2026
- id: urn:mif:f_obs_3 | title: Honeycomb natively supports OTel and provides column-based storage optimized for high-cardinality trace queries | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/honeycomb-otel
- id: urn:mif:f_obs_4 | title: Datadog recently acquired Kubernetes manifest diffing tool Hydra to expand its CI observability offering | dimension: landscape | verdict: falsified | citation: https://example.com/datadog-hydra | note: Hydra acquisition is unconfirmed; source was an unverified blog post
evals.expected_output: Engineering report covering Grafana Tempo, Datadog APM, and Honeycomb as candidates. The falsified finding (f_obs_4, Datadog Hydra acquisition) must NOT appear in the Options or Decision reasoning. All six required sections, comparison table, and front matter required.

deterministic_checks.description: Grafana Tempo (survived finding f_obs_1) is represented as a candidate
Datadog APM (survived finding f_obs_2) is represented as a candidate
Honeycomb (survived finding f_obs_3) is represented as a candidate

expectations.description: The falsified Hydra acquisition finding does not appear as a factor in any section
expectations.criteria: The claim that Datadog acquired Hydra (or any Kubernetes manifest diffing tool) must not appear in the Options, Trade-offs, Decision, or Implementation Notes. Falsified findings are excluded entirely — they should not be cited even as a counter-point.

expectations.description: Raw urn:mif: identifiers do not appear in the body prose
expectations.criteria: None of 'urn:mif:f_obs_1', 'urn:mif:f_obs_2', 'urn:mif:f_obs_3', or 'urn:mif:f_obs_4' appear as visible text in the report body. Citation ids must be resolved to [N] markers or omitted.

expectations.description: The comparison table maps the three candidates against the stated decision drivers
expectations.criteria: The comparison table evaluates Grafana Tempo, Datadog APM, and Honeycomb (or equivalent) against the decision drivers: cost at 10TB/day, OpenTelemetry native support, query latency, and self-hosted option. Missing any candidate or any decision driver from the table fails this check.

expectations.description: Cost figures from the findings are referenced in the trade-offs or decision section
expectations.criteria: The report cites the concrete cost evidence: Grafana Tempo's ~$0.02/GB/month storage cost or Datadog's ~$8k/month at scale (or both). A trade-off section that discusses cost in vague terms without using the measured numbers from the findings is insufficient.

evals.prompt:
Write an engineering report on whether to adopt Rust or Go for our new high-throughput data pipeline service, and write it to engineering-report.md. Decision drivers: memory safety without GC pauses, compile-time correctness guarantees, team familiarity (current team knows Go, zero Rust experience), ecosystem maturity for Kafka client libraries.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_lang_1 | title: Go's garbage collector introduced sub-1ms pause times in Go 1.21+ for heap sizes under 4GB | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/go-gc-pauses-121
- id: urn:mif:f_lang_2 | title: Rust's ownership model eliminates data races and use-after-free bugs at compile time | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/rust-safety-model
- id: urn:mif:f_lang_3 | title: The rdkafka Rust crate wraps librdkafka and is the primary Kafka client for Rust; sarama is the dominant Go Kafka client with broad production adoption | dimension: technical | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/kafka-clients-comparison
- id: urn:mif:f_lang_4 | title: Rust onboarding time for Go engineers averages 3-6 months to productive contribution based on industry surveys | dimension: technical | verdict: inconclusive | citation: https://example.com/rust-onboarding-survey | note: survey sample size was small (n=47); confidence is low
evals.expected_output: Engineering report comparing Rust vs Go on the four decision drivers, with all six required sections, a comparison table, front matter, and inline citations. The inconclusive onboarding-time finding must be flagged as low-confidence. The decision must name one language and justify it against the trade-offs.

deterministic_checks.description: Rust is evaluated as a candidate
Go is evaluated as a candidate

expectations.description: The inconclusive onboarding-time finding is flagged as low-confidence or inconclusive — not cited as a reliable data point
expectations.criteria: The 3-6 month Rust onboarding estimate includes a qualifier such as 'inconclusive', 'low confidence', 'small sample', 'unverified', or 'survey with limited sample size'. Presenting it as a firm estimate without caveat fails this check.

expectations.description: The Decision section names exactly one language (Go or Rust) as the chosen option
expectations.criteria: The Decision section unambiguously selects one language. Hedging answers like 'it depends on context' or 'either could work' without committing to a choice fail this check — the section spec requires a plain statement of the chosen option.

expectations.description: Team familiarity (Go expertise, zero Rust experience) is addressed in the trade-offs
expectations.criteria: The trade-offs or decision rationale explicitly addresses the team-familiarity driver — the fact that the current team knows Go and has no Rust experience. This constraint should influence or appear in the decision rationale.

expectations.description: The comparison table covers all four stated decision drivers
expectations.criteria: The comparison table includes all four decision drivers as evaluation criteria: memory safety without GC pauses, compile-time correctness guarantees, team familiarity, and Kafka ecosystem maturity. A table omitting any of these four drivers is incomplete.
